comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
The need for a protagonist in human nature informs the central principle of Object-Oriented Design. Humans think in terms of objects that we touch and mutate. Mutation is inconvenient for reasoning when it happens all over the place, which is why it's eschewed in programming as a fragile thing.

In the UI space mutation needs to be felt by humans. It's table stakes for an effective UI.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: That's an example of a classic tradeoff where you have to know when ergonomics take precedence over adhering 100% to the metaphor. We make similar tradeoffs when we design the Lyn Rummy card game. We know the physics of the kitchen table, but because we can only approximate the kitchen table physics on the screen, we adjust at times to the ergonomics of the screen.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I think that's actually your paraphrase of what I said, but I will take credit anyway haha.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: The phase change is super key.  That's the mutation again. Humans live in a world where everything changes around them when acted upon. We expect the same physics on the computer, even though we know it's an abstract representation.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: That, again, was YOUR key insight. That's why your solution was novel and not really prompted by me (other than my insistence to run multiple iterations).

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Don't worry, though...we will be talking about the VM again very soon in a completely non-UI context.
